---
type: primer
provenance: "synthesis"
---

# Making It Matter {.primer .unnumbered}

*Silhouette: A game can be perfectly built and still leave the player cold; this is the craft that stops that.*

<!-- Primer: orientation to the Story, Character & Voice family. Light authorial / Mentor voice. -->

## The Terrain

This family is the craft of narrative. The other families work in rules, in clues, in props, in the shape of time, in the people round the table. This one works in the part the player thinks they came for. The voice in their ear. The world they walk through and the person they meet inside it, and the moment that finally lands. Everything else in the guide is in service of this. A puzzle is fair so that the story it gates can be felt. A reset is reliable so the same room can move the next group too. When the rest of the work is right and the narrative is thin, the player walks away having solved a thing rather than lived one. [synthesis]

The work has two halves, and they run together at every scale. The first is belief. A voice that holds from the first line to the last. A world whose rules do not contradict each other. A genre whose promise is kept and a machinery tucked out of sight. All of it asks the player to take the fiction as real for the length of play. The second half is care. People who feel like people. Conflict with a real cost, and an emotional beat the story has earned the right to land. Belief makes the world solid. Care makes the player want to stay in it. A game can win one and lose the other. A flawlessly consistent world full of names nobody loves is believed and unmourned. A wrenching scene set in a world that keeps tripping over its own rules moves the player once and then loses them at the next contradiction. [synthesis]

Belief comes first, and not by preference. Care is built on top of it. The player cannot grieve a character they do not believe in, cannot feel the weight of a choice in a world whose own logic keeps slipping. So the order of the work is fixed even though the two halves run at once: earn the belief, then spend it on the moment you want to matter. Break belief at the wrong instant and the care you spent the whole way building leaks out through the crack. [synthesis]

The same question runs through every scale, from one line of dialogue up to a whole genre's contract. Does the player believe it, and do they care? A single exchange between two characters answers it. So does the decision to keep a world's magic consistent across three books. The scale changes; the question does not. [synthesis]

::: {.callout-tip}
### Mentor Wisdom
A player never cares about a world you have not first made them believe in. When a scene falls flat, the instinct is to raise the stakes with a bigger threat or a louder death. Resist it. Ask instead whether the player believes the world the stakes sit in. A death lands because the person who died was real for an hour first. They were real because the small things held. The voice stayed theirs. The world kept its rules. Nothing on the page reminded the player it was made. Fix the belief and the care often arrives on its own. Pile on stakes the player does not yet believe in and you get melodrama, which is care asked for before it was earned. [synthesis]
:::

## Key Mechanisms

**Voice and language.** The anchor of the family is [Character Voice](../articles/story-character-voice/character-voice.qmd): what makes a person on the page sound like themselves and no one else, the test you can run by covering the names above the dialogue. The talk itself, where two people speak past and around what they mean, is [Dialogue Craft](../articles/story-character-voice/dialogue-craft.qmd). The two dials under all narration, how close the camera sits and how the sentences breathe, are [Prose Patterns & Point of View](../articles/story-character-voice/prose-patterns-and-point-of-view.qmd). The promise that the voice you start in is the voice you finish in, which gets harder the moment more than one author writes the world, is [Voice & Register Consistency](../articles/story-character-voice/voice-and-register-consistency.qmd). And getting the reader what they need without a lecture, trusting them with what you leave out, is [Exposition & Subtext](../articles/story-character-voice/exposition-and-subtext.qmd).

**The believed world.** Keeping the interface and the machinery inside the fiction, so the player meets a world and never the gears, is [Diegetic Design](../articles/story-character-voice/diegetic-design.qmd). The story a room tells before anyone speaks is [Environmental Storytelling & Setting as Character](../articles/story-character-voice/environmental-storytelling-and-setting-as-character.qmd). The rules a made-up world keeps so the reader stops noticing it was made are [Worldbuilding Patterns](../articles/story-character-voice/worldbuilding-patterns.qmd), and the discipline that stops those rules contradicting each other across a long run is [Canon Management](../articles/story-character-voice/canon-management.qmd). The first worldbuilding the reader ever meets, one syllable at a time, is [Naming Craft: Characters & Locations](../articles/story-character-voice/naming-craft-characters-and-locations.qmd).

**What the player feels.** The engine under every scene, a character who wants something and the thing in the way, is [Conflict Patterns](../articles/story-character-voice/conflict-patterns.qmd). The moment a story has earned the right to make you feel something is [Emotional Beats & Investment](../articles/story-character-voice/emotional-beats-and-investment.qmd). A choice between two goods or two bads whose cost arrives later and adds up into a portrait of who you played is [Moral Dilemmas & Consequence Chains](../articles/story-character-voice/moral-dilemmas-and-consequence-chains.qmd). The team of specialists and the perfect plan tested the moment it meets reality is [Heist & Caper Patterns](../articles/story-character-voice/heist-and-caper-patterns.qmd). And a love story the player helps write, where the other person is allowed to say no, is [Romance & Relationships](../articles/story-character-voice/romance-and-relationships.qmd).

**The genre contracts.** Each genre signs a different promise with its audience, and the map of those promises is [A Taxonomy of Genre Conventions](../articles/story-character-voice/a-taxonomy-of-genre-conventions.qmd), which also names the promise that breaks first when a genre goes interactive. From that map, seven genres get their own treatment. The genre that dares the reader to solve it first is [Mystery & Detective Fiction](../articles/story-character-voice/mystery-and-detective-fiction.qmd). Making a reader afraid on purpose, and keeping them afraid without wearing the fear out, is [Horror Conventions](../articles/story-character-voice/horror-conventions.qmd). Building a world that does not exist and then keeping faith with its rules is [Fantasy Conventions](../articles/story-character-voice/fantasy-conventions.qmd). One impossible thing accepted on purpose, then followed everywhere it leads, is [Science Fiction Conventions](../articles/story-character-voice/science-fiction-conventions.qmd). A real past worn lightly enough to walk through rather than study is [Historical Fiction](../articles/story-character-voice/historical-fiction.qmd). Writing for the young at full craft, where no lesson gets preached, is [Children's & YA Conventions](../articles/story-character-voice/childrens-and-ya-conventions.qmd). Every genre draws on a shared cast of stock roles, and the honest craft of bending a familiar one into something fresh lives in [Tropes, Archetypes & Variations](../articles/story-character-voice/tropes-archetypes-and-variations.qmd).

**The solo-journaling form.** One form turns the whole question inward, where a single player writes and the writing is the play rather than a record of it. The genre and its shape are [Solo Journaling RPGs: Genre & Form](../articles/story-character-voice/solo-journaling-rpgs-genre-and-form.qmd). The single sentence that has to make a stranger want to write the next page is [Prompt Design in Solo Journaling](../articles/story-character-voice/prompt-design-in-solo-journaling.qmd). And the written object that remains after the session, the thing you keep, is [The Journal as Artefact](../articles/story-character-voice/the-journal-as-artefact.qmd).

**The cast and the audience.** The supporting cast, the people the player meets rather than plays, and how to give each of them a voice that holds, is [NPC Craft & Voicing](../articles/story-character-voice/npc-craft-and-voicing.qmd). And pitching the prose and the stakes to a reader of a known age without ever talking down to them is [Audience & Age Targeting](../articles/story-character-voice/audience-and-age-targeting.qmd).

## Related Exemplars

*Disco Elysium* (ZA/UM, 2019) is character voice as the whole game, a cast so distinct that the skills inside the detective's own head argue in voices you can tell apart with the names covered. *Kentucky Route Zero* (Cardboard Computer, 2013) keeps its machinery so far inside the fiction that the player meets a world and never the gears. Ursula K. Le Guin's Earthsea holds a magic with rules strict enough that the cost of a name is felt across six books without once contradicting itself. And *The Wretched* (Chris Bissette, 2019) is the solo-journaling form at its sharpest, a deck of prompts and a tower of blocks where the writing left behind is the only record that the play happened at all.
